lesson.menu_desc: Deducing the past: must/might/can't have -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Reading People & Situations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 18 -- Reading People & Situations

extra_audio.text: First, Pricila bows to a colleague in Japan, and he looks surprised. Then she worries: she must have done something wrong. Next, a friend explains the local custom, so she realizes she can't have known the rule. After that, she sees he meant no harm, and she gives him the benefit of the doubt. So she smooths things over with a warm smile, and the awkward silence melts away.

listenings.text: Something funny happened on my last trip, and I still think about what it really meant. I was in Japan, having dinner with a colleague I had just met. I handed him my business card with one hand, the way I always do, and he went completely quiet. There was this long, awkward silence. At the time I thought, oh no, I must have said something wrong, or he must have taken offence at something. I read the whole situation as a disaster. But looking back now, with what I know, he can't have been angry. He was far too polite for that. He might have simply been surprised, because in Japan you offer a card with both hands, as a sign of respect. I had no idea. I jumped to conclusions, when really it was just a small culture clash. He must have realized I was a foreigner who meant no harm, because a minute later he smiled and we carried on. So now, when someone abroad goes quiet on me, I try to read between the lines. They might have a different custom. They can't have meant to be rude. I give them the benefit of the doubt, and nine times out of ten, the misunderstanding just melts away.

Hey Pricila, it's me. You asked me about that strange moment you had in Japan, so let me explain, because honestly, you can't have known the rule. It's a tiny custom, but it matters a lot over there. When you give someone your business card, you're supposed to hold it with both hands and a small bow, and the other person reads it carefully before putting it away. If you hand it over with one hand, the way we do back home, the other person might think you're being a little casual. But here's the thing: nobody who knows you would ever think you meant to be rude. He must have understood straight away that you were a visitor, and visitors can't have learned every local rule. That's just a faux pas, the kind every traveller makes. My advice? When you're abroad and someone reacts in a way you don't expect, don't assume the worst. Body language and customs change from place to place. People rarely mean any harm. Read between the lines, give them the benefit of the doubt, and a smile usually smooths everything over. You handled it just fine. Talk soon.

paras: Travel throws strangers together, and strangers are hard to read. A pause that means nothing at home can feel like an insult abroad; a gesture meant warmly can land as cold. The traveller who survives these moments is not the one who never makes a faux pas, but the one who knows how to think about them afterwards. And the grammar of that thinking is the past modal of deduction: she must have misunderstood me; he can't have meant it rudely; they might have a different custom. We did not see what happened inside the other person's head, so we deduce it -- with more or less certainty -- from the little we did see.

The trap is to jump to conclusions. When a host goes quiet, or a waiter frowns, or a colleague does not shake your hand, the quick, anxious mind fills the silence with the worst story: I must have offended them. But the same evidence usually supports a kinder reading. He can't have been angry -- he was smiling a moment later. She might have simply been tired, or shy, or unsure of the language. To read between the lines is to hold two or three of these explanations at once, and to lean toward the generous one until the facts say otherwise.

That generous lean has a name: giving the benefit of the doubt. It does not mean being naive. It means remembering that body language and custom shift from one country to the next, that an awkward silence is rarely an accusation, and that most people, most of the time, mean no harm. When a culture clash does happen, the skilled traveller does not lose face by panicking. They smooth things over with a question and a smile -- and quietly file the moment away as one more thing they now know how to read.

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about misunderstandings abroad?
choices: The best travellers are the ones who study every local custom in advance so they never make a single mistake.
When a situation abroad is confusing, we should deduce the kinder explanation, give people the benefit of the doubt, and read between the lines instead of jumping to conclusions.
Past modals of deduction prove that other people are almost always being deliberately rude to travellers.

items: The writer says a gesture meant warmly can sometimes land as cold in another culture.
t
A gesture meant warmly can land as cold.
According to the writer, the best traveller is the one who never makes a faux pas.
f
Not the one who never makes a faux pas, but the one who knows how to think about them afterwards.
The writer warns that the anxious mind tends to fill a silence with the worst story.
t
The quick, anxious mind fills the silence with the worst story: I must have offended them.
The writer says giving the benefit of the doubt means being naive about everyone.
f
It does not mean being naive; it means remembering that custom shifts and most people mean no harm.

matching.title: Match each expression to its meaning
words: To give the benefit of the doubt
defs: To understand a situation wrongly, especially someone's mood or intention
A small social mistake that breaks an unwritten rule of politeness
To feel hurt or insulted by what someone says or does
To decide what something means too quickly, before you have the full picture
To choose to believe the kinder explanation when you are not sure
A moment of friction caused by different customs or values
To understand the real, hidden meaning behind what is said or done
To calm a tense situation and make the bad feeling go away

rephrase.title: Deduce the past with the right modal
items: You're almost certain she misunderstood you. (must have)
She must have misunderstood me.
It's impossible that they meant it rudely. (can't have)
They can't have meant it rudely.
It's possible he was confused by the custom. (might have)
He might have been confused by the custom.
You're almost certain the card was offered with one hand. (passive: must have been)
The card must have been offered with one hand.
It's impossible that the waiter was being deliberately rude. (can't have been)
The waiter can't have been being rude on purpose.

answerkey.title: Reveal the deduction answer key
list: They can't have meant it rudely.
He might have been confused by the custom.
The card must have been offered with one hand.
The waiter can't have been being rude on purpose.
answerkey.note: Past modals of deduction = how sure you are about the PAST. must have + past participle = you are almost certain it WAS true. can't have + past participle = you are almost certain it was NOT true (never 'mustn't have' for this). might have / could have + past participle = it was possible. The form is always modal + have + past participle, and it does NOT change for he/she/it (never 'must has'). Passive: must/might/can't have BEEN + past participle (the card must have been offered).

analyse.title: Close reading
items: The writer says the skilled traveller is not the one who 'never makes a faux pas'. Why does the writer value how we THINK about a mistake more than avoiding it?
The text contrasts 'I must have offended them' with 'he can't have been angry -- he was smiling a moment later'. How does the SAME evidence support two different deductions about the past?
What is the difference, according to the writer, between giving someone the benefit of the doubt and simply being naive?
